0: Joint data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment for environmental impact reduction in broiler production systems
Z. Payandeh a K. Kheiralipour a, M. Karimi B. Khoshnevisan C

<list>a Mechanical Engineering of Biosystems Department, Ilam University, Ilam, Ilam, Iran 
b Mechanical Engineering of Biosystems Department, Arak University, Arak, Markazi, Iran 
C Department of Agricultural Machinery, University of Tehran, Karaj, Alborz, Iran</list>
1: Article history: Received 2 December 2016 Received in revised form 23 March 2017 Accepted 24 March 2017 Available online 30 March 2017
Keywords: Chicken Energy use Data envelopment analysis Environmental impacts Life cycle assessment
2: In recent years, livestock production sector has significantly grown associated with increasing of envi- ronmental impacts. In the present research 90 broiler farms in Isfahan Providence, Iran, were considered to be evaluated in terms view of environmental impacts. The aim of the present research is reducing environmental impacts of the studied farms by data envelopment analysis. By the method, efficiency of the farms was determined and inefficient farms were improved to reach efficient level through reducing energy and resources use. Before and after comparing all farms, eleven environmental impacts of the farms were calculated using life cycle assessment method. The results showed that the used energy in farms can be reduced by 10.16%. The contribution of food production to environmental impacts was especially high in almost all environmental indicators. As a result of energy use improvement, the environmental impacts were reduced by 12-57%. The highest reduction was seen in ozone layer depletion, abiotic depletion, marine aquatic ecotoxicity and human toxicity as 57.37, 31.32, 27.59 and 26.73%, respectively. The research methodology and obtained results can be used to reduce environ- mental emissions through managing the resources and energy use in broiler production systems. 2017 Elsevier Ltd. 3: The livestock sector has been expanded rapidly in recent de- cades and it is projected to be continually grown as a result of increasing trend of food demand. One of the main parts in this dynamic and growing sector is broiler production [1]. The Middle East is the highest per capita consumer of broiler meat in the world. Increasing trend of population growth, urbanization and purchas- ing power has been caused to increase broiler meet consumption.

The worldwide broiler chicken industry has been continuously grown over the last 40 years. Broiler meat is an important source of high quality proteins, minerals and vitamins to balance the human diet [2]. The global broiler production in 2013 was estimated over 96 million tonnes of meat and after beef and pork is the third most consumed meat. At the moment, Iran is the seventh broiler pro- ducer in the world with annual production of 2 million tonnes [3].
The livestock production has significant role in emission of greenhouse gases. There is estimated that broiler meat and egg

production sectors emit annually 606 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent as about 8% of the total greenhouse gases emitted in livestock sector. About 343 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent in 2013 is the share of broiler meat production [4]. To evaluate the environmental impacts of systems, life cycle assessment (LCA) method is a powerful tool that is vastly used in agriculture sector. In this method, the life cycle of a system is considered as well as predetermined its inputs and outputs. The life cycle perspective allows a comprehensive assessing procedure, i.e. the possibility of taking the whole production chain into account to improve the environmental performance of products [5]. LCA method has been used to calculate the environmental im- pacts of broiler production systems. Pelletier used LCA to predict macro scale environmental impacts along the US broiler supply chain. In his research, contribution of food provision in some impact category was calculated [6]. Ewemoje et al. assessed envi- ronmental impacts of production processes from hatchery to point- of-lay using LCA method. Their results showed that total contri- butions to global warming were 9.708 kg of CO2 equivalent, 11.34 kg of CH4 equivalent and 0.2 kg of NO2 equivalent. Contributions to acidification were 2.713 X 10-1 kg of SO2 equivalent, 1.948 X 10-3 kg

of NH3 equivalent, 2.167 X 10-3 kg of NOx equivalent and energy consumption was 59.79 KJ per bird [7]. Another research was re- ported by Gonzalez-Garcia et al. to investigate environmental im- pacts of broiler production. They determined contribution of environmental impacts in all categories with significant contribu- tions to eutrophication potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), photo-oxidant formation potential (POFP) and global warming potential (GWP) as 99%, 98%, 99% and 93% of total contributing emissions, respectively [8].

Data envelopment analysis (DEA) has been used for evaluating the performance and efficiency of industrial and agricultural sys- items. DEA is a nonparametric method to estimate the relative ef- ficiency of decision making units (DMU), frontier DMUs and optimal values of each DMU to reach frontiers [9].

Different studies have been conducted for assessing agricultural production systems by DEA approach in view point of energy. Chauhan et al. applied this method to determine the efficiencies of rice production farms. Their results revealed that about 11.6% of the total input energy could be saved [10]. Begum et al. calculated technical efficiency of commercial broiler farms using DEA method. They considered constant returns to scale (CRS) and variable returns to scale (VRS) model to estimate the efficiency of DMUs [11]. Heidari et al. determined technical efficiency (TE), pure tech- nical efficiency (PTE) and scale efficiency (SE) of energy use in broiler farms as 0.90, 0.93 and 0.96, respectively. In addition their results revealed that about 11% of the total input resources could be saved [2].

As stated above, some researches were conducted to evaluate environmental impacts of broiler production systems by LCA method and in some other researches DEA method was used to estimate energy efficiency and energy use reduction in the systems. But in the literature, a research was not reported to reduce envi- ronmental impacts in broiler production system. So, the aim of the present research was reducing environmental impacts of broiler production systems in Isfahan Providence, Iran. In this study, DEA method was applied to estimate and optimize efficiency of energy consumption in the systems. Energy reduction of the systems was calculated by subtracting the energy use before and after compar- ison. Then, LCA method was used to calculate the environmental impacts of the systems before and after comparison and calculate the potential reduction of the impacts by subtracting the emitted impacts before and after comparison.

8: A flowchart of calculation steps in the present research has been shown in Fig. 1.

The first step was started after data collection. In this step, the real amounts of used inputs in all broiler production farms were determined. After that, the real amounts of energy consumption and the environmental impacts in the broiler production farms were calculated according to the consumed input recourses by LCA method. All broiler production farms were improved in terms of energy consumption by DEA method and then the reduced amounts of energy consumption and also energy reduction in the farms were calculated. The reduced amounts of input resources were calculated according to the reduced energy consumption. Finally, the environmental impacts were calculated based on the reduced input resources by LCA method and then the reduction of environmental impacts were determined.
9: In this study, 90 broiler farms in Isfahan, Najafabad and Nain Township, the most broiler producers in Isfahan Province, Iran,
Fig. 1. The main calculation steps of environmental impacts reduction.

were randomly selected to be studied. Isfahan Province is located within 30.65 north latitude and 51.67 east longitude. The required data were gathered by questionnaires and interview with the farm owners in August-September growing period, 2014. The average of growing period was 58 days. The used resources (inputs) in the studied growing period were: total amount of food, diesel and electricity consumptions and total working hours of labors. The outputs of the studied broiler farms were meat and manure.
10: Data envelopment analysis (DEA) is a non-parametric method which the relationships between all inputs and outputs are taken into account simultaneously [12]. The method compares the stud- ied broiler farms as DMUs, and finds out the relative efficiency of the farms and examines their positions in relation to the frontiers. DEA method includes two models: CCR (Chames, Cooper and Rhodes) and BCC (Banker, Chames and Cooper) models [13]. CCR model is constant returns to scale and BCC model is variable returns to scale method. Each of these models is divided to output oriented and input oriented approaches. Input oriented approach means that an inefficient DMU must decrease input to become an efficient one, while the output is fixed. Whereas, the output oriented method fixes the input levels and increase the output values.
In DEA method, three efficiencies are estimated as technical efficiency (TE), scale efficiency (SE) and pure technical efficiency (PTE). Technical efficiency (or global efficiency) is determined by

evaluation the performance of DMUs relative to each other [13]. Scale efficiency is the potential productivity which is obtained based on the achieved optimal size of a DMU [14]. Pure technical efficiency or local efficiency is the technical efficiency that removes the effect of scale efficiency [15]. The difference between three efficiencies has been explained in Fig. 2.

Two DEA models have been shown in Fig. 2. The MN line that passes from the extreme data points represents a constant return to scale. Each DMU on MN line is efficient one and its efficiency is equal to unit. Also, pure technical efficiency of the DMUs on break line (P1, P2, P3 and P4) is equal to unit. Having both technical ef- ficiency and pure technical efficiency, scale efficiency of DMUs can be estimated. The scale efficiency of a DMU is equal to unit if its technical and pure technical efficiencies are equal to unit. So, P2 DMU is efficient but other DMUs are not and they are called as inefficiency DMUs. The three efficiencies can be explained by following equations [7]:

The input recourses of broiler farms before and after comparison were considered to determine the environmental im- pacts by LCA method.

12: Data envelopment analysis (DEA) is a non-parametric method which the relationships between all inputs and outputs are taken into account simultaneously [12]. The method compares the stud- ied broiler farms as DMUs, and finds out the relative efficiency of the farms and examines their positions in relation to the frontiers. DEA method includes two models: CCR (Chames, Cooper and Rhodes) and BCC (Banker, Chames and Cooper) models [13]. CCR model is constant returns to scale and BCC model is variable returns to scale method. Each of these models is divided to output oriented and input oriented approaches. Input oriented approach means that an inefficient DMU must decrease input to become an efficient one, while the output is fixed. Whereas, the output oriented method fixes the input levels and increase the output values.
In DEA method, three efficiencies are estimated as technical efficiency (TE), scale efficiency (SE) and pure technical efficiency (PTE). Technical efficiency (or global efficiency) is determined by

evaluation the performance of DMUs relative to each other [13]. Scale efficiency is the potential productivity which is obtained based on the achieved optimal size of a DMU [14]. Pure technical efficiency or local efficiency is the technical efficiency that removes the effect of scale efficiency [15]. The difference between three efficiencies has been explained in Fig. 2.

Two DEA models have been shown in Fig. 2. The MN line that passes from the extreme data points represents a constant return to scale. Each DMU on MN line is efficient one and its efficiency is equal to unit. Also, pure technical efficiency of the DMUs on break line (P1, P2, P3 and P4) is equal to unit. Having both technical ef- ficiency and pure technical efficiency, scale efficiency of DMUs can be estimated. The scale efficiency of a DMU is equal to unit if its technical and pure technical efficiencies are equal to unit. 15: Life cycle assessment is an approach allows for accounting environmental impacts related to a product, process or activity. LCA approach includes four phases based on ISO 14040 and ISO 14044: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation of results [5,24]. In the present study, LCA method was used to calculate environmental impacts before and after improvements of energy use of the broiler farms by DEA.
16: Goal and scope definition is the first phase of LCA that explain the purpose of the study, the expected product of the study, the system boundary and the assumptions. In the present study, LCA method was used for calculating the environmental impacts of production process of broiler farms before and after DEA compar- ison. The system boundary was farm gate including consumed fuel, electricity and food and emissions associated with live chicks (Fig. 3). Broiler breeder production, slaughter, chicken packaging and salon construction were not considered in this research.

The functional unit of this study was one tonne live mass of broiler. This functional unit is in agreement with previous studies [9,11,25]. Since both energy and environmental impact reduction were simultaneously investigated, mass allocation method was considered in the present study [11,26].
17: The second phase of LCA study is life cycle inventory (LCI). Various input resources and environmental impacts were

quantified in this phase. The actual and ideal inputs of the farms were considered as LCI data. Further information (for production of food, electricity, transportation, etc.) was taken from the SimaPro 8.0 Software Database. Fuel, electricity and food were considered as input resources in the LCA study and outputs were chicken meet and manure. Same to other studies, cleaning agents used for disinfection of poultry salons were neglected in the LCA study [27,28].

Electricity is utilized for different purposes in the studied broiler production farms such as: ventilating, lighting and water pumping. Electricity supply has been delivered from the grid region. Two kinds of fuels, 99% natural gas and 1% Mazut are used in electricity grid of Isfahan Province. The environmental impacts of each elec- tricity supply method were calculated according to fuel consump- tion percentage.

The used fuel for heating system in the studied broiler farms was included diesel and natural gas. In this study, the fuel consumptions were high because Isfahan Province is located in a dry region. Emission factors for using diesel fuel and natural gas were considered based on [24]. The USLCI Database was used in this study [29].

The share of maize and soybean was more than 90% of the broiler foods. The information related to foods was taken from the SimaPro 8.0 Software Database. In this study, crop production was not included in the system boundary and it wasn't considered in assessment process. The largest emission of live chicks comes from the litter manure. N20 (direct and indirect) and CH4 are emission factors of litter manure that were determined based on IPCC guideline [24].

20: In this study, the CCR and BCC models were considered to evaluate technical, pure technical and scale efficiency of poultry production. Efficiency values of the studied farms were presented in Table 2.

The mean values of TE, PTE and SE were calculated as 0.91, 0.96 and 0.92, respectively. It is clear that the mean efficiency of all farms were less than unit that shows inefficient amount of resources and energy consumption in the studied farms. The highest value of standard deviation (0.13) was belonged to technical efficiency with a range of 0.49-1. This indicated that technical efficiency better than other efficiencies shows the variation and also revealed that more farms loss the energy or inefficiently use energy and so need more cares to improve their operating practices for enhancing en- ergy and resources consumption efficiency.

In the literature, TE, PTE and SE of broiler farmers of Yazd Providence, Iran, were reported as 0.90, 0.93 and 0.96 [2], technical efficiency of poultry egg production as 0.873 [12] and TE and PTE for broiler farms as 0.70-0.73 [8].
According to Duncan Multiple Range Test, the studied DMUs were significantly separate by different efficiency classes (Fig. 4).

The result of CCR model showed that between total of 90 farmers, only 46 farmers were relatively efficient (TE = 1) and the remaining were inefficient, i.e. their efficiency value were less than unit. Whereas the result of BCC model indicated that 57 farmers were efficient (PTE = 1). Because of the lower number of indicated efficient DMUs based on CCR model, the TE values were considered for comparison of the inefficient DMUs.

26: In this section, the mean values of environmental impact of all broiler farms were calculated before comparison (actual). The corresponding ideal values were calculated after DEA comparison. Impact reduction was calculated as the difference between actual and ideal impacts. The environmental impacts before and after comparison and impact reduction was presented in Table 5.

Some previous studies reported global warming, eutrophication and acidification indicators related to emissions from chicken production farms to produce 1 tonne of chicken meat in different countries. Dasilva et al. estimated the emission in France for the mentioned indicators as 2216 kg CO2 eq, 13.8 kg PO4 eq and 28.7 kg SO2 eq, respectively. Dasilva et al. reported their findings in Brazil

as 2058 kg CO2 eq, 14.4 kg PO2 eq and 34.5 kg SO2 eq, respectively [25]. In Finland, Katajajuuri et al. achieved 2079 kg CO2 eq, 2.1 kg PO4 eq and 35 kg SO2 eq, respectively [30]. Pelletier studied emission of broiler farms in USA and obtained indicators as 1395 kg CO2 eq, 3.9 kg PO4 eq and 15.8 kg SO2 eq, respectively [9]. Williams et al. expressed emission values for the indicators in United Kingdom as 1800 kg CO2 eq, 14 kg PO4 eq and 25.9 kg SO2 eq, respectively [31]. In another research, Leinonen et al. studied emission of United Kingdom and expressed 3087 kg CO2 eq, 14.2 kg PO4 eq and 32.7 kg SO2 eq, respectively [32].

The global warming, eutrophication and acidification indicators for production of 1 tonne chicken meat in the present research were equal to 5782.380 kg CO2 eq, 9.881 kg PO4 eq and 35.755 kg SO2 eq, respectively. The amounts of eutrophication and acidifica- tion indicators in Iran are same as corresponding values in different studied countries. But, global warming is higher in compare with other countries that must be decreased by considering better managing use of input resources.
Contribution of inputs to environmental impact categories for producing 1 tonne chickens before comparison has been shown in Fig. 5.

As can be seen in Fig. 5, the contribution of food to environ- mental impacts derived from the studied farms was especially high, except for human toxicity and marine aquatic toxicity indicators. The share of food in the abiotic depletion, depletion of fossil re- sources, global warming, ozone layer depletion, fresh water toxicity, terrestrial toxicity, photochemical oxidation, potential acidification and potential eutrophication indicators was equal to 75.1, 41.8, 58.3, 64.6, 72.6, 46.5, 25.3, 58.9 and 94.6%, respectively. Maize and soybean are most important ingredients of food in broiler farms. They had highest contributions in all categories due to its cropping stage.
The impact of livestock food on human toxicity and marine aquatic toxicity was low (1.43 and 1.08%, respectively) but the share of live chicken on the indicators was high.

The most important input in the potential of global warming was food due to releasing carbon dioxide into the air (91%). Equipment (steel) had the highest share in ozone layer depletion indicator (52.78%). Contribution of the heating systems to envi- ronmental impacts was 22.4% in acidification potential, 2.73% in eutrophication potential, 1.54% in ozone depletion and 22.3% in global warming. Emissions of heating systems in the studied broiler farms were correspond to burning natural gas and diesel.

Methane was the main source of emission into the air in ozone layer depletion indicator. In acidification potential, ammonia with amount of 13.49 kg SO2 eq (39%) had the highest greenhouse gas emissions and in eutrophication indicator, nitrate with amount of 5.61 kg PO4 eq had a share of 57% of the emissions to the water.

As reported by several researchers, food is the most important input for increasing environmental impacts associated with farm- related activities [9,27,30,33,34]. da Silva et al. studied the emis- sions from poultry production in Brazil and France. They concluded that food input had the highest emissions in the global warming, soil toxicity and eutrophication indicators. In toxicity indicators, food had the highest share in Brazil, but live chicken had the highest emission in France due to NH3 emissions. Due to high use of fossil fuels, food had high effect on global warming indicator. The use of nitrogenous fertilizers for food in Brazil caused to increase the toxicity indictors [25].

Nielsen et al. investigated the greenhouse gas emissions of poultry production in Denmark. They reported that the main factor in emissions of global warming potential was the food input with a share of 91% because of high use of fossil fuels [26].

this sector. They reported that food input is the biggest impact point in broiler chain with emission amount of 1885 kg CO2 eq [33]. According to the results of the present and previous studies there was concluded that the food input has the highest emissions. The use of fossil fuels in the production of agricultural products increases the amount of emissions. Also in food production step or any step that fossil fuel is used, the environmental impacts can be minimized by using high fuels efficiency equipment. Renewable energies are good alternative for fossil fuels that reduce the envi- ronmental impacts.
Environmental impact values after comparison were presented in the fourth column of Table 5. Also, the comparison of present and reduced environmental impacts for all DMUs, have been shown in Fig. 6.

As seen in Table 5 and Fig. 6, amount of all studied indicators was decreased after comparison. For example, depletion of fossil resources by the studied farms was equal to 40924.976 MJ before comparison which decreased to 35358.585 MJ after comparison. Considerable impact reduction can be detected in depletion of the ozone layer. The present ozone layer depletion (ODP) was 4.225 kg CFC-11 eq that can be reduced to 1.801 kg CFC-11 eq (57.37% reduction). The global warming indicator was 5782.380 kg CO2 eq before comparison whereas the corresponding reduced emission will be 5004.346 kg CO2 eq after comparison. So, emission reduc- tion of GWP can be 778.034 kg CO2 eq. Human toxicity reduced from 41447.050 to 30366.805 kg 1.4-DB eq. Acidification can be
reduced by 12.89% and eutrophication reduced from 9.881 kg PO4 eq to 8.620 kg PO4 eq (12.80% reduction).
36: In the present research, the energy use and environmental im- pacts of poultry production in Isfahan, Iran, were evaluated before and after energy use improvements by DEA. The total energy use can be reduced by 10.16%. Environmental impacts were signifi- cantly reduced after comparison. The indicator including, abiotic depletion, abiotic depletion (fossil), global warming, ozone layer depletion, human toxicity, fresh water aquatic ecotoxicity, marine aquatic ecotoxicity, terrestrial ecotoxicity, Photochemical oxidation, potential of acidification and eutrophication were decreased by 31.32, 13.60, 13.46, 57.37, 26.73, 16.12, 27.59, 17.43, 17.38, 12.89 and 12.80%, respectively. As food input has the highest contribution to the emissions due to high amount of fossil fuels consumption, it is needed to apply efficient management method to optimize food consumption, decrease food losses in the broiler farms and decrease fuel consumption in food producing farms. Also, improving mechanization and management level in broiler farm to reduce fuel, electricity and other inputs are recommended. The results of this study can be useful for farmers and agricultural or- ganizations to reduce energy and resource consumption and environmental emissions. Also, more technical and economic as- pects can be considered to improve the management of poultry
